text:
Personal announcement about the direction I'm taking this account:

I've been writing regularly here for something like eight years now.

It started one morning when I woke up and told myself: what if I started to journal online and wrote a few posts in English every day? This shouldn't be difficult as long as I'm living a life worth writing about. I'll probably get more eloquent in the process, improve my English, and potentially make some friends.

On the small desk of my old share-house room in Tokyo, there was a computer, an orange, and a few books.

That’s how “Orange Book” was born.

My writing probably evolved quite a lot throughout the years, but the core idea has always been “write what you want/need to read” and “self-reflect on your own unique experiences, find your own words, write them down, and maybe they will resonate with a few other people out there as well.”

I value my own privacy, so I naturally decided to create a faceless account, write in an impersonal voice, and leave aside most of the details of my personal life. I also didn’t want to spoil my own intergenerational novel that I'd been working on for many years now that is based on the stories of my family.

Unfortunately, it also increasingly felt like I was writing in a voice that AI could replicate anytime; but also, after iterating on so many different drafts of my book, I slowly understood that the “final version” was going to take a very different shape.

It has many characters, many locations, many perspectives, many voices that aren’t mine, and it was getting obvious that a lot of the details of my personal journey that I had kept “secret” were never going to make it to the book anyway, so I thought I might as well share them with the people who wanted to know.

Going forward, I am shifting most of my writing to the subscription space, and although the topics will remain similar - health, wealth, education, relationships, talent, purpose, and more recently, parenting - I will adopt a much more personal voice, far closer to who I am in real life, and hope to attract readers who care more about the real-life stories than the lessons I once extracted from them.

Recently, it sometimes felt like I was becoming a caricature of myself anyway, and people who have been following me for many years probably noticed how my output had been decreasing. I tried to renew myself as a writer last year by interviewing other people, but it turns out that I am more of an essayist than a journalist; and hopefully, I’ll be able to call myself a novelist soon.

After nearly 15,000 public tweets, I’m making a shift towards pursuing a much more personal and authentic voice on this platform.

Big thanks to all the people who have been reading me throughout the years, I’m very grateful for the connections I’ve made here, and I hope to see some of you on the other side.

Orange Book

Thoughtful people who don’t have the courage to cut ties with people who only care about themselves get drained out of their positive energy as the years pass, and never strike any meaningful success or get lucky regardless of how talented and hard-working they might be:

Most people waste too much time explaining themselves to people who never had their best interests at heart. Time explaining what you do is time not doing it. You want to get things done, you need to learn how to ignore most of the world:
@Boluwatifeolad7 Thank you so much for the kind words and for reading during all these years, really appreciate! 🧡

Too many people talk about "conviction" when they are actually just too intellectually lazy to question the current course of their life trajectory. If the years pass and life isn't getting better at all, it's not conviction, it's wishful thinking that needs to stop:

Any long-term success involves countless iterations of yourself, and more unlearning and relearning that you can recall, and the compass to be trusted is internal: when you are in the right direction, your mind should get calmer, envy should disappear, and you should be able to question your current self without doubting who you could become.
Money doesn't change people; a lack of principles and character does:

If we somehow all had access to a simulator that allowed us to experience life as a rich person, we'd waste far less time on the same traps that our ancestors already fell in, we'd figure out much more quickly what it's like to be a truly wealthy person and lead a wealthy life.
Intergenerational wealth isn’t just about what you leave to your kids, it’s also about what you leave to your parents while you still can:
Your parents are the only people in the world who need nothing from you to genuinely love you; and what you do with that information says a lot about the kind of person you are.
There are actually many signs that you are currently in the right direction in life:

And as you eventually look back, it will all seem very obvious that the challenge was always to stay calm when it felt like life was targeting you from all sides simultaneously: when you felt like you had no other choice was actually the test that you were meant to overcome.

You suddenly get luckier once you strike the right amount of "unemployment." 

Being constantly busy chasing opportunities ironically makes you miss the most important ones.

Success boils down to being early and right, and that only happens with the courage to look like a fool:
@laraghavan Well said. The soft spot is probably a situation along the lines of "hardship that doesn't feel so painful that you lose your mental clarity, but still force you to move."
@QXsToo Sent you a DM!
When you refuse to change as the world changes, it's not integrity, it's not sticking to your principles; it's mostly repeating the common pattern of old people who are stubbornly wrong.
Most of your "mental struggles" actually come from having too much free time and not having enough courage to deeply commit to any difficult goal.
If you are not serious about maintaining a consistently high-energy version of yourself, you are not serious about any of your goals.
Insecure parents often raise kids that are full of self-doubt; building and nurturing your own self-confidence is a parental responsibility.

Constantly talking about wealthy and successful people who don't even know that you exist is not only a sign of low intelligence, but also a sign of profound lack of self-respect, as you should have much better to do with your life.
Your growth never stops if you let your fears be your life compass instead of what you stay away from.

Many people who gave up on getting wealthy say they don't care about money; many people who gave up on finding love say they don't want to get married; many people who gave up on building a loving family say they don't want to have kids; behind most people who "don't want" something are actually idealistic and romantic souls who got disappointed, people who no longer want to get hurt.

A lot of success boils down to whether you can figure out how to build and stay in an environment where you will consistently retain enough quiet time alone to reflect on who you truly want to be when untainted by groupthink and peer pressure, then invest in yourself accordingly.
Anxiety is just unrealized potential.

People who think they don't have enough time actually usually don't have enough depth of thought, they are impulsive and emotional, they end up making a lot of unnecessary mistakes, and spend the rest of their lives fixing them instead of working on real problems.
Your relationships are a reflection of your level of self-respect.
Getting regularly angry and annoyed at trivial things that don’t matter is mostly the sign that you have nothing meaningful ongoing with your life.

It may sound like these standards are way too low, but you look around, and you realize that parents who are simply not constantly arguing with each other in front of their kids are actually a small minority, and that if you are treating your spouse with respect and love, as it should be, you are already doing a great job as a parent.
Being afraid of change mostly means that you’ve been comfortable for way too long already.

Abundance of opportunities comes to people who enjoy playing the long-term games through the decades; scarcity of opportunities happens to people who are always afraid of committing and following through anything at all.

In a game, if you get a bit bored, you increase the difficulty level and you start over; in real life, if you feel a bit empty, you build a family with the goal of being a good parent and you start over; harder means more fun and meaningful, you just need to play more skillfully.

Stop writing about writing, and start the actual writing.

Stop talking about money, love, purpose, and start actually committing to something.

Stop worrying about the worst-case scenarios all the time, and start actually living the only life you have.

Progress comes from action, not from endless self-reflection.
Chasing prestige and power is one of the most common ways people choose to run away from their insecurities.
When you are truly investing properly in yourself, “today” is always the “worst” your life is ever gonna be.
@JoeJustice 🧡

Most people who think they don’t have time to work out and take care of themselves are precisely in such a position because they didn’t work out and take care of themselves earlier in life.

They ended up never developing the mental clarity necessary to make the smart long-term decisions that would have allowed them more freedom as they got older.

Most of your anxiety disappears once you have a comfortable financial safety net, healthy habits you are diligent about, a loving circle of family and friends, and a long-term vision that naturally makes you grow as a person year after year.
Most people who spend their time complaining about everything are actually people who never faced any real problem.

“You will get it once you are older,” is in retrospect, the kind of intellectual shortcut that people who were confused about how to live their lives and who never grew the mental clarity and eloquence to understand why they were where they were, love to use.

Now that I’m older, I get it indeed, that your life is the compounded decisions and the efforts that you started ten or twenty years ago, and people actually get exactly the life they deserve, it just takes more than average intelligence and memory to understand the causality of the sequence of your choices:

you look around, and most people barely remember the articles and videos they consumed a few weeks ago, let alone the conversations from a few years ago, let alone their “life-changing” turning points from ten years ago.

In doubt, hang out with older people who are actually happy with their lives, it’s obvious when you look at them, they seem much younger than their age, they are surrounded by love every day, and they will actually have advice worth listening to, not the common cynical, pessimistic, and jaded view that most old people who didn’t get to experience a wholesome life rooted in freedom have.

At some point in life, you’re gonna be dealing with health issues, if not your own, your grandparents, your parents, your children; and everyone who went through this knows how much energy it takes away from you, and wouldn’t want to inflict it on anyone else; which is why not having the discipline to maintain a healthy lifestyle usually means that you don’t care much about anyone else.

Anxiety signals that you are competing where you shouldn’t. When you are smart, you focus on what you do best, you don’t mindlessly work hard out of pride. The best path you could walk makes you feel free: you are playful, relaxed, confident; you are where you are meant to be.
Building self-confidence is the art of closing the gap between what you claim you want to do and what you actually do.

A great family starts with a great marriage, and a great marriage starts with a great relationship with yourself: that's when you will naturally attract people who are also in a great relationship with themselves, that's when you can finally start focusing on goals that matter.
Most of your current frustrations come from the younger version of yourself who refused to make the necessary changes; be kind to your future self and don't repeat the same mistake.

Smart people love the intellectual stimulation that comes from exchanging perspectives with smarter people and keep getting smarter; average people feel very insecure about being around people who can read them like an open book, so never do it, and never grow much as a result.
Anxiety is the gap between your current standards and the standards you should have.
If you feel at peace with who you are, get to spend as much time as you want with the friends you like and the family you love, and don't need to work for money, you are already extremely wealthy.
People who take themselves too seriously never figure out the peace of mind that comes from steady growth because they are too afraid of being wrong in public.
Great friends always unapologetically point out what you can't notice about yourself that you really need to notice.
The most important truths, the ones that lead to the most life-changing opportunities, are found in the gap between how you see the world and how everyone else sees the world.
Don't expect life to get less stressful, just train who you are and get mentally stronger.

People who don't want to make the effort to think about what they actually need in life tend to mindlessly chase money, thinking that they can always figure things out later, after they got rich, and that's usually only a good recipe for walking your whole life on someone else's path.
Nothing brings as much anxiety as not having a clear goal and watching your years passing by in a meaningless way.

What most people call anxiety is usually lack of a clear goal, lack of courage to actually act, lack of honesty with themselves about the life they truly want, and in so many cases, it’s even all three at the same time.
People don't struggle to teach emotional control to their kids, they struggle with their own, and you can't teach what you can't do.

Most professional failures are actually personal relationship failures.

You will generally learn more slowly if you are surrounded by the wrong people.

You will also waste so much time in unnecessary drama instead of actually advancing your goals.

If you’re gonna have high standards for the quality of your work, you should also have high standards for your relationships.

Parenting is never something you can outsource, if your work is taking up all your time, you are effectively choosing to be an absent parent, and yet so many parents somehow managed to convince themselves that they were doing it for their kids and that they were “good parents.”
If you can just figure out how to stay high-energy, intellectually curious, mentally calm, and emotionally present around your kids, you are already doing much better than most parents.

So many parents can’t even show love and respect to their spouse in front of their kids, they are constantly stressed out, worried, angry, but somehow also have the arrogance to talk about raising emotionally healthy children.
Your anxiety is the accumulation of the shots you never took because you talked yourself out of them due to your lack of courage.
If you don't need to work for money, but somehow can't figure out how to live a happy life either, the problem isn't that money can't buy happiness, it's that money can't buy good taste.

Your ability to build the life you want isn't defined by your occasional moments of inspiration or strokes of genius; it's defined by what you do with plain sheer discipline during your average and bad days.
There is nothing sadder than leading a life where you are never wrong, where you never have to question and renew yourself, it means you've been spending it all in the wrong directions.

You will keep growing as a person as long as you maintain the habit of doing, at least once every day, something you've been reticent or scared of doing; it could be reaching out to a stranger you've curious about, traveling to a place you've always been interesting in, reading a book that always intimidated you, having a conversation in a foreign language, investing a bit of money in a field that you don't understand, publishing your imperfect thoughts online, getting married and starting a family despite your fears of commitment, getting rid of old unhealthy habits that had become a big part of yourself, stopping and staring a little bit in a world that keeps making you feel like you can never stop running.
And you will feel at peace with yourself and with the way you've been spending your life as long as you keep growing.
Getting older is also the realization that genuine friends who think about you and care are extremely rare.

Average minds often spend their time fixing problems they could have avoided in the first place, and they are somehow always very proud of it; smarter people make sure the problems never exist, never mention it, and no one ever notices either.
Anger is a common manifestation of the frustration of not being who you want to be.
When they say "I don't want it," most people actually mean "I'm scared of trying my best and still fail to get what I want, hence I'd rather not even try at all."

If you value respect and trust, but live in a low-trust and low-respect society, just move to another place where your common sense will be aligned and not debated, you’re gonna save so much time and energy, your relationships will become effortless, life will get so much better.
Anxiety is your mind begging you to change your lifestyle.
Being poor ironically makes everything more expensive, in money, in health, in energy, in time.

Every generation, you have technological breakthroughs that redefine the rules of the games we play in society; and every generation, you have people who refuse to relearn who they are, because they are too invested in beliefs that are now wrong, because they married a fixed identity instead of an evolving identity, because they are too obsessed with their nostalgic feelings to see and adapt to the new world they live in; make sure you don't belong to that crowd.
The day you are no longer afraid of "disappearing" in order to live a quiet and private life with the people you love, you've won the game.

Assume that it's always possible to be a loyal spouse, a loving parent, a devoted child, a thoughtful friend, a productive citizen, an avid learner, a disciplined athlete, an unconstrained artist, an independent thinker, a wise human being that doesn't take themselves too seriously; then learn how to be constantly more efficient with your time, leave your judgments and ego aside, leverage your capital fearlessly, get rid of your pride and use all the tools available, and go live your greatest life.
It's actually very recommended that you keep "disappointing" those who never really wanted the best for you.
Being busy all the time kills your creativity, your relationships, and your ability to notice what matters.

The only honest advice you will get from successful people is to stop asking them what once worked out for them (because it wouldn’t work the same way for them anymore, let alone for you) and just focus on your own strengths, your own accumulated efforts, your own unique journey.

Many people ironically become who they dislike, because you become what you focus on, and too many people spend their time thinking about their unpleasant interactions with others when they should rather be thinking about who they want to be and what kind of life they want to build.
@james_riney @GearoidReidy 🤭😂
Overreacting and panicking over trivial things that won’t matter in the long run is mostly the sign that your life has been far too comfortable until now.
Life never gets easier, grow some skin, get mentally stronger, that’s the only choice you have.
Anxious overthinkers are just people who lack the courage to act.

You're gonna be wrong many times along the way, but you will find your direction; that's far preferable to never being wrong (and never being right either), and living your whole life worried about everything all the time.

At birth, you didn't choose whether your family was rich or poor; but as an adult, with every passing year, your life becomes more and more a reflection of your decisions, and at the end of your life, being poor, in every sense of the term, mostly means that you lived life with a poor mindset.
Nothing is more frustrating than discussing with people who are smart enough to think, but never brave enough to act.

It's clear when you look at people who manage to get what they want from life, that they are not necessarily the smartest or the most talented, but they are definitely quick to act, they don't overthink unpredictable details, and they don't mind a bit of uncertainty along the way

It's so common to only get busier and busier as you get older and as "life is happening," with job constraints, with kids, with old parents, with health issues; and as such, a test of intelligence is how much freedom to live your life on your own terms you manage to retain.
Never be proud of being busy all the time.

Build relationships with people who have their private life in order, they tend to not suddenly disappear for no reason, and you will be able to compound your relationships with so much more depth over the decades.
When you care about status, you start performing for others, which is the recipe of living your whole life without ever experiencing either true freedom or your full potential.

RT @undefined: 2026 is going to be my “Alpha School” year where I go back and learn any core stem fundamentals I didn’t master in school.

Things that have felt hard to grasp bc I may have missed a day when I was sick in 7th grade and never caught up.

Electrical engineering, some concepts in physics, core math stuff etc. 

Convinced that it will take me relatively very little time with AI but will open my mind up in a big way. 

Too many of us only go further with what we’re comfortable with which is usually the subjects we felt we understood when we were ~12-21.

Knowledge compounds.

And there’s never been a better time to fix the gaps. AI changes everything.
Being very judgmental of everything all the time is mostly the sign that you have too much free time and not working on ambitious enough goals.
When you "protect" your children from difficulty, you are actually setting up a difficult life for them.
Never trust someone who cannot clearly tell you what they want from life; confused people with too much free time are always full of drama.

A great education always involves a lot of quiet, unstimulated time alone; that's when you finally connect with your own original thoughts, that's when you clarify how you truly want to live your life.

So many wealthy people are worried about their kids becoming spoiled, lazy, entitled; but when you look around, it’s clear that growing up poor often makes far more damage when it comes to bad habits, self-discipline, and self-control.

Anyone who grew up in a rough neighborhood and who “made it” knows that they are an outlier, and that so many of their former classmates literally ended up in jail, with addictions, or with mental health or anger issues that destroyed their future relationships.

Most people who think they are not smart enough to build the life they want are actually struggling with emotional control: constant anxiety makes you dumb, constant anger makes you confused, constant fear makes you stagnate.

One of the most common signs of arrogance is when you spend most of your time mindlessly consuming entertainment that won’t improve your life whilst simultaneously expecting life to get better. Life gets better once you start treating your time with enough respect, never before.
You see a lot of rich people who struggle with their marriage, their family, their relationships, because accumulating money is much easier than accumulating wealth.
Envy is life telling you that you should stop getting distracted by other people’s lives and start working on your own.
A sign of success and internal fulfillment is when you are no longer obsessed with “changing your life” anymore.

A lot of “luck” boils down to maintaining the environment and routine that nurture the most high-energy version of yourself, whilst simultaneously hanging out with other high-energy people who also have a clear idea of what they want from life.
Intellectual curiosity doesn’t guarantee wealth, but lack of intellectual curiosity guarantees poverty.

The biggest opportunities you miss throughout your life aren’t measured financially, they are the experiences you never knew you could have had, that would have transformed you into the healthiest, calmest, happiest, most confident version of yourself.

Lack of success when it comes to building the life you want basically boils down to three points:

1) lack of honesty with yourself to face who you are and how much effort in the right direction you’ve made or (or haven’t made, yet)

2) lack of self-discipline and emotional control to stay the course and stick to the plan when life is testing your convictions, and it will

3) lack of courage to make the tough decisions:

leaving some old baggage behind and moving on to where you actually belong; 

making more time to build relationships with people with whom conversations are both effortless, stimulating, reinvigorating;

unlearning all the parts of yourself that you never liked but got comfortable living with, in order to rebuild yourself “cleanly” into someone you actually respect.
Anxiety happens when you know deep down that you should be doing better. It's the voice that screams when you've betrayed yourself and lowered your standards.
The only test of intelligence that matters is whether you navigate your own life on your own terms.
The less you take yourself seriously, the faster you grow as a person.

Every new year is the opportunity to trim down what has been a mental burden on you for too long already: underwater investments that you kept out of wishful thinking, side projects that distracted you from what you do best, relationships with people who never cared like you did.

Anxiety is when you are smart enough to know the life you need, but not brave enough to build it: your lack of courage makes you overthink every step along the way, you make no progress, you resent your own inaction, and mental peace never comes to you.
Most people who think that relationships are complicated are actually struggling to fix their own insecurities first.
Growth begins once you have the honesty to admit that you are not satisfied with the current version of yourself.

A lot of "talent" boils down to assuming that you have no talent, no particular good luck, that no one will help you for no reason, and that miracles don't happen.

That's when you will shift to the right mindset of taking responsibility for who you are and where you are in life.

That's when you will start clarifying and cultivating the right long-term habits and efforts.

That's also when "good luck" and miracles may start happening.

Reality doesn’t care that you don’t have time to catch up with the knowledge that you should be up-to-date with if you want to intelligently navigate a fast-changing society. Ironically, the busier you are, the busier you will get. Make time to regularly relearn your assumptions.
Good parents are rare because so many adults are still children who still refuse to take responsibility for where they are in life, let alone another living being.
The first step to raise mentally healthy children is to be a mentally healthy parent.
Acting like a victim is a mental disease.
You somehow make a lot of people feel “uncomfortable” when you just mind your own business and chase an ambitious vision for your life.

When you don't take proper care of your own health, you are actually sending the message that you also don't care about your kids being able to grow up with parents who are high-energy, mentally stable, emotionally present; and as they are modeling their behavior on yours, you are actually actively failing their education.

Relationships get better once you no longer have to worry about money because you can finally have genuine conversations with no agenda, no time limit, no obsession around how to turn every idea into a business.

The end of life is when you start thinking that you are already too old to start anything new, take foolish risks, or invest unapologetically in yourself: the world is full of people who are alive without ever truly living, but it doesn’t have to be that way for you.
Marrying someone you love and having plenty of time to raise your kids together, that’s wealth.
A lot of success in life boils down to how good you are at unapologetically ignoring all the things that won’t improve your life.

There are three types of people who suddenly "disappear": those who are ashamed of who they are, those who have everything they want and understand the value of being low profile and private, and those who have a newborn to attend to.

It's obvious when you compare "old people" from very different environments with very different standards of efforts, that people with active lives, who keep learning, who keep pushing themselves physically and mentally regardless of their age, tend to remain young much longer.

The "right direction" in life is a sequence of doing what you've been afraid of, over and over again; that's when growth happens naturally, that's when serendipity is amplified, that's when you gain self-confidence and build the life you want.
@jetsetter328 Stay if you don’t mind who you are becoming, move if you do.
If you actually understand the value of writing, “the algorithm” or “AI” are the least of your worries.

It’s scary how the attitude of some people suddenly change when they know you have money or connections, and actually quite sad when you just wanted a genuine, non-codependent friendship, but they suddenly “disappeared” when they couldn’t get what they wanted from you.
Anxiety is also what happens when you lack the courage to speak your mind when you know you should, and have been living your whole life that way.
If you become more cynical as you get older, it actually means that you've been failing at life.

Not being able to maintain healthy habits as you get older is the sign that you are not serious about your goals and your relationships (or in so many cases, that you have no goal at all, or don't care about the people around you.)

Leave alone the people who are so confused and directionless that they think that harming themselves with unnecessary substances and stimulations is a fun way to live life.

There is no debate that the most high-energy and mentally clear version of yourself, who is capable of supporting a family or employees, requires a healthy lifestyle.
Life actually keeps sending you the very exact wake-up calls you need to turn your life around, but most people fail to hear them because they are too busy listening to their rationalizing mind.
Bad habits are a sign of arrogance, you are explicitly stating that you don’t need to be the best version of yourself to reach your goals.
Stagnation is a sign of cowardice.

People who claim that they are pushing themselves "for others" but who somehow cannot take care of their own health properly, usually just become a burden on everyone around them once their body and mind inevitably break down.

Refusing to leave an environment where you starve to have stimulating conversations with smart people who are serious about their goals slowly kill your ambitions and make you lose all self-confidence. A lot of "bad luck," anxiety, struggles are actually choices that people make.

People with no particular "talent," but who possess unwavering self-belief, and who keep putting in the work decade after decade, always outperform "smart" people who mostly use their intelligence to overthink everything or doubt themselves.

People who didn't grow much as they got older are people who lacked the courage to build the habit of hitting their limits over and over again.

Growth is the art of putting yourself in situations where you will still fail despite doing your best, then learning how to enjoy them.
Most people who think they lack talent are actually vastly underestimating the amount of obsession and training that are required to become "talented."

If you were not dealt great cards at birth, the first thing you need to discard is your pride, you are not going to achieve the same as people of equivalent talent and work ethic if you started ten steps behind: whenever you feel like something is "below your line," that's when you actually have an opportunity to beat the odds.

You want your kids to grow self-confident, you need to let them make the mistakes that you yourself had to make in order to grow, stop solving all their problems for them. If you grew up with parents who believed in your ability to fix your problems, you were very lucky already.
Being proud of being busy all the time is effectively the same as being proud of working very inefficiently.
@matt_eluwa Thank you! Mostly from conversations with friends and daily life.
Most "mental struggles" are just people addicted to living in their own mind instead of actually having the courage to improve who they are, act every day, get things done.

If you are rich, no need to pretend to be poor in front of your kids; if they are smart, no need to send them in a rough neighborhood just to "teach them" about life; you don't need to manufacture "difficult challenges" for your kids; at any level, there are plenty of very challenging goals that not accessible unless you were already coming from a certain set of "privileged" circumstances; let them go after these goals instead, that's how their potential will meaningfully grow.

A common mistake of people with a lot of financial resources: they think that parenting is something they can outsource. That’s how you end up with people who grew up with every possible advantage, but who lack the emotions and warmth of someone who grew up in a loving family.

True wealth starts with becoming who you want to be, because that's the only way to be truly mentally and spiritually at peace; and most people never get there, they just unawarely end up becoming who society wanted them to be; that's why traveling and living abroad in very different cultures is so important, it makes you notice and helps you differentiate your societal conditioning from who you actually want to be.
Real friends will always point it out when you are about to make a mistake that they already made.
Overthinking is a sign of average intelligence. Keep investing in your mind until clarity becomes your default state.
Your mind will feel annoyed by the smallest inconveniences as long as you aren't focused on ambitious enough goals.
Figuring out the right direction in life isn't a big deal if you can clearly and accurately imagine the older version of yourself on your deathbed, with all the regrets that you can still avoid.

People who never nurtured their intuition and ability to think independently love recipes and methods that they can follow mindlessly, because they get the feeling that they are learning something effortlessly, when reality is that there is no such thing as effortless learning.
Effortless learning is superficial learning, and life only rewards deep learners.

The less you take yourself seriously, the "luckier" you will get in life. Get rid of your pride and fear of embarrassment. Put yourself in situations where you will grow regardless of whether you feel like it or not. No one is watching, no one cares. You're gonna live your life, die, and be vaguely remembered for one or two generations if you had a loving family, that's about it.

Mentally weak people are just people who had the privilege to have other people sort out their problems for way too long. It's impossible to not grow mentally stronger when you have to stand alone and take responsibility for where you are in life.
If your spouse is rational and mentally stable, you actually already won the lottery.

Getting regularly involved in foolish arguments and energy-wasting drama usually means that you have too much free time. 

Most people’s problems are in their mind, and they disappear once they are finally focused on goals that actually matter.
Everyone talks about how they have less free time once they have kids, no one talks about how you also learn how to be much more focused and more efficient with your time.
Being able to spend a lot of time with your kids, teach them, play with them, watch them grow up, is an underrated sign of wealth.
The more options you have, the more courage you need, to make choices and finally move forward.

Not American and not eligible, but it’s a great initiative to encourage more long-form writing and deeper thinking, which is how our species has been thriving.

People follow incentives, and as someone who has been writing on the platform for nearly a decade now, it’s clear to me that visual content has been more and more predominant on X/Twitter, which seems to be turning into another Instagram/TikTok, and no longer a place where outliers and “weird” communities could thrive like it used to.

I always found the trend very sad, as I initially came to Twitter to connect with communities and people from all around the world on an intellectual level, and not just to be fed another algorithmic feed full of mainstream memes and viral videos.

I still believe that the whole monetization strategy is a mistake, although it unfortunately seems inevitable in terms of long-term financial viability for the company, but a lot of authentic artists and writers, and all the people who have been contributing to the Internet for free for many decades now, haven’t been doing what they are doing for money, and actually get quite offended when others assume they can just be bought.

I did make lifelong friends through this platform, and only wish for its long-term success (hence why I’m still around and speaking up) — but back to the initial subject, I’d encourage everyone who hasn’t yet developed a writing habit to start.

I know that we now live in the noise of AI generated content, and in the capitalist games revolving around productivity and hustling, some people look at you as if you are insane when you take the time to slowly craft your own sentences,

but as someone who has been writing and rewriting countless drafts of the same novel for many years now, and who initially started to write on Twitter as part of my routine as the equivalent of my mental work out, it became really clear that there is no bigger beneficiary to the act of writing but you, the writer.

Mental clarity, self-understanding, calmer mind, deeper empathy for others, creativity, sense of intellectual freedom, these all come to you naturally once writing is a daily habit;

and also, we are likely all better off living in a society where, instead of infinitely-scrolling AI-fed zombies, everyone is a writer capable of thinking independently and with depth.

A few lessons learned from 2025:

1) Life has a way to give you what you want after you give up on chasing it directly.

2) The best conversations happen serendipitously between people with no agenda.

3) If you are not becoming more humble as you get older, you are running in the wrong direction.

4) If you married someone thoughtful who genuinely cares and who is committed to building a better life together, you’ve already won the lottery.

5) Kids completely change your routine, but it doesn’t mean you will be less productive, you just need to be more efficient with your time and focus on the right things.

6) If your environment doesn’t reflect your vibes, the faster you have the courage to move on, the earlier you will start getting lucky.

7) You can always take a good guess at how much time left you have with your parents, it’s up to you to make the most of it, for them but also for you.

8) People who don’t grow much are just people with too much pride and not enough humility to question their methods.

9) Everyone should have one hobby that forces them to be the healthiest and physically strongest version of themselves, and another hobby that forces them to maintain mental sharpness and bold creativity.

10) Lack of courage to speak your mind ruins all relationships regardless of your intentions.

11) The most talented people don’t take themselves seriously, otherwise, they wouldn’t have made all the necessary mistakes to get where they are.

12) Being well-dressed and aesthetically pleasing is underrated, for yourself but also for others; most people who claim it’s narcissistic just don’t have the discipline to make the efforts.

13) People with clear and ambitious goals, and a loving family, don’t have the time or the energy for any unnecessary drama.

14) Introducing people who might get along great with each other is an underrated habit.

15) You are more resilient than you think, especially when you do things for people you genuinely love.

Happy new year to everyone! All the best for 2026!

RT @undefined: Competitive Spirits or Gentle Dreamers?

Twenty years ago, I had a female friend. She was a bright, energetic, and capable woman who ran her own company. We often got excited about all kinds of topics, went out for tea, and talked for hours. Later, we both had families and had children. We weren’t partners or spouses—just platonic friends who could talk about anything. But once the topic turned to raising kids, we discovered a big difference in our values.

“You have to raise them to be tough and hate losing,” she said.
My view was the opposite: “I want to raise them so they don’t become obsessed with winning.”

When this difference became clear, all the small mismatches I had felt in our communication suddenly connected. “Ah, so that’s what it was,” I thought. It felt like a fog lifting. I became strangely calm, and a natural distance peacefully grew between us.

Winning is a form of comparison. It exists only because there are others, groups of others, and a specific measuring stick used to judge higher or lower, bigger or smaller, better or worse. You become aware of other people, get on the roller coaster of competition, win fiercely, enjoy winning, or even make winning the purpose of your life. Other people’s evaluation is always there. Competition with others is always there.

Maybe even if the child hasn’t decided yet, being born into certain parents already puts them into a kind of competition. People who survive hardship with a “I’ll show you!” Mentality—who purposely avoid calming their mind—sometimes become economically successful.

My image of an ideal mentality was different. I see relative evaluation as a system intentionally created so someone can control someone else for some purpose. I don’t want children to be ever forced into that race, let alone from the from the get go of their life. I want them first to find something they can be absorbed in, so absorbed that the presence of others fades away. I don’t want their childhood or youth to be shaped by competition.

You could also call it a political way of living versus a creator’s way of living. Once, I heard a story from someone connected to a European high maison that made perfect sense to me. The story went that the founding family’s madame would quietly observe young artisans spending time together. And like pulling weeds to grow a fruitful orchard, she would “thin out” certain personalities among the apprentices.

She would intentionally remove the young artisans who showed political or strategic behavior in relationships, even though they were craftsmen. It might sound cruel, but maybe she had her own way of sensing the balance between “creating good work” and “advancing one’s career.”

Recently, photos of minister-level meetings between Japan and a major Asian power have been circulating. We should keep in mind that such images might be taken out of context or used to stir sensational reactions. Still, the photo clearly showed a representative of that major power standing with his hands in his pockets, body angled in a dominant pose, looking at the Japanese delegates as if they were a blackboard.

The video was quite shocking. Their posture sent a clear message: I am stronger. I am above you. 

Seeing that image reminded me of my conversation with my old friend. If competitive DNA is built into human instinct, maybe there will always be those who compete. Is it a kind of division of roles? Do culture and art grow only after a nation wins in international competition? That’s what I found myself thinking while watching that video.

There is the lens of force and the lens of aesthetics. In Japan, warriors who had military power longed for culture and built tea rooms, while nobles hired warriors as protectors. It seems that both force and aesthetics shape society and make history move.

How do I want to live? How do I want my children to live? What kind of country do I want mine to be? Flip a coin and choose: heads or tails?

9) They never understood how to take advantage of the serendipity of their environment. They didn't understand that a lot of talent was merely accumulated efforts in the right environment. They didn't understand that a lot of good luck was merely hanging out long enough in the right environment. They didn't understand that a lot of loving relationships were merely nurturing an authentic and consistent version of themselves throughout the decades in an environment that would appreciate it.

10) They never acted with a long-term mindset. They always assumed that they didn't need much money, or that it was too late to start investing, instead of thinking about their parents, or their potential children and grandchildren. 

They assumed that they might not even live in the same neighborhood in a few years. They assumed that there were plenty of fish in the sea. They assumed that they could always make new friends anytime, instead of fostering and compounding deeper connections with people they knew. The Internet gave them the illusion that they were always a few clicks away from a life-changing encounter with better people, but they slowly realized that the older they got, the less true it became.

They assumed that it was too late to start a new hobby, a new project, a new life, instead of investing in themselves as if they were to live forever. They never understood how the best life that they could live would always involve a playful version of themselves who had mastered the right amount of detachment, and who had learned how to enjoy the process, the craft, the simple and foolish moments in life.

2) They were living in their mind. They spent their time judging the success of others instead of training their own talents. They kept making excuses about why they couldn't do whatever they were afraid of trying. They never realized that life was short, that no one really ever cared, that they would miss out on life not because of their lack of intelligence or talent, but because of their lack of courage.

3) They never understood the importance of a healthy body. They thought they had mental struggles, but they actually had physical deficiencies. They never unlearned their bad habits because they never distanced themselves from all the people who considered these "normal." They took it personally and got offended if anyone suggested any lifestyle change. They never experienced life in a clear-minded and high-energy version of themselves, and somehow started to believe that it was normal to feel sluggish, moody, gloomy once in a while.
Lack of growth is just lack of courage to take real responsibilities.

In the past few years, Japan has experienced a lot of tourists coming from abroad, and it unfortunately led to a surge of negative sentiment towards foreigners as evidenced by the most recent elections (Sanseito, a party whose slogan was "Japanese first," won a lot of seats.)

As always, it's also a reflection of economic difficulties: for example, the price of real estate in many parts of Tokyo has nearly doubled in just a few years because of speculators (both Japanese and foreigners, but foreigners will mostly be blamed, sometimes unfairly, but sometimes for rightful reasons when they don't even live here and yet bear a certain arrogant demeanor because the yen is currently weak.)

When I first came here, I was welcomed with so much kindness and love by so many Japanese people, so it's honestly a bit sad to see how the general perception can get skewed negatively because of a minority ill-mannered and/or greedy, transactional people who struggle to understand why you should be respectful of the local culture when you are a guest in another country.

I will write below additional posts during the next few months/years, but I truly hope that the Japan that has welcomed me a decade ago hasn't definitely disappeared.

2) Accumulate a wide range of experiences and meet a lot of people, don't be scared of surrounding yourself with people who are much better than you, have a point of comparison to figure out where you are mediocre, average, good, great

3) Physically move to a place where you will be able to learn from people who can make the great greater, don't underestimate the importance of long-term serendipitous encounters

4) Once in a while, you will bond with someone effortlessly, they might be from another country, they might be decades younger or older than you, but keep these people in your life, they are rarer than you think

7) Call your parents and grandparents while you still can, you will only start truly understanding what they went through and start feeling grateful once you have kids yourself, "you will get it when you are older" is actually underappreciated wisdom

8) Marry someone that you are happy to love and support, someone who figured out what they wanted from life and who has the courage to make choices, someone you choose to share the inevitable ups and downs of the journey with, a perfect marriage requires tolerance to imperfections

Investing and writing are very similar:

In the beginning, you are a nobody with nothing to lose: you don't mind being completely wrong, you go high-risk high-reward, you keep coming up with bold takes and provocative theses, you know that's the only path to stand out anyway.

Then, as you accumulate more financial and social capital, you are afraid of losing your comfortable lifestyle, you don't want to disappoint people who rely on you, you also soften your writing, you dodge topics that might alienate your audience or offend mentors you admire, you move more cautiously, you lose your edge, you are not failing, but you are not soaring either.

Finally, you wake up, you remember the mindset that took you from nothing to something, you remember the thrill of your early gambles, you realize that being prudent is actually very expensive in time and in life, you understand that moving forward, even when you are wrong, is cheaper than standing still, you retrieve your beginner's mindset, you remember how it felt like to wake up in the morning and be truly excited about something, you cultivate enough mental strength to not feel exhausted by the heavy expectations of others, you relearn how to have fun writing and building your own ambitious vision.

Mental health is when you wake up every morning with a clear purpose, you know what you want from life because you had the courage to make choices, you have intellectually stimulating goals that make your mind grow year after year, you have learned how to effectively ignore everything that has nothing to do with your life, and you know how to focus on what will inevitably get you closer to who you want to be and to the life you want to have.

If you keep consuming junk food, you will obviously become low-energy, mentally numb, and physically out of shape.

The same way, if you keep consuming "mental" junk food, you will become irritated, anxious, and depressed.

Too many people are obsessed about what they should do in order to get healthier and calmer, when the biggest part of the work is in what they should stop doing.

A mentally healthy mind is a mind that knows what to not read and consume, what conversations to not take part in, what games to not play.

Music will teach you that you don't sound like you think you sound.

Sports will teach you that you don't move like you think you move.

Investing will teach you that society doesn't work the way you think it works.

Writing will teach you that you don't think as clearly as you think you do.

Great friendships will teach you that it's actually rare to truly connect with someone else.

Maintaining a healthy lifestyle will teach you that you neither need to be angry, anxious, or depressed.

Building a loving family will teach you how many worries and hurdles your parents, grandparents, and ancestors overcame to give you an opportunity to experience life.

Committing to a long-term meaningful purpose will teach you that you can grow your talents and achieve so much more than you think.

You won't achieve much if you are always worried about everything and everyone around you; if you are serious about your ambitions, move to a high-trust neighborhood, build relationships with people who know how to take care of themselves, you will suddenly get more productive.

The smarter you are, the more courage and emotional control you need, because you won't be able to stop being aware of all the worst possible outcomes, but you can learn how to focus on the best possible outcomes, and find daily joy in creating a path to get there.
Most smart people actually need less thoughtful introspection and more “irrational” initiatives.

Self-confidence is just a direct consequence of the habit of forcing yourself to do what you are scared of doing, over and over again, regardless of the excuses your mind keeps making up in order to choose the more comfortable path.
Many people spend the first half of their lives chasing what society convinced them that they wanted, and they spend the second half regretting what they no longer have the energy to do.
A lot of anxiety disappears once you keep the promises you've been making to yourself.

Money is not the goal, it’s just a stepping stone that grants you the freedom to pursue your real goals.

The actual purpose of money isn't to buy things, it's to not be bought.

Anxiety is the sign that you aren’t acting proactively enough: you shouldn’t have time to live in your mind, you should be busy preparing the roads towards your big goals, you should be training your body, your mind, your skills, you should be trying, reflecting, doing, living.

True wealth is simple:

A healthy body, a relaxed mind, a loving family, enough financial freedom, living in a neighborhood you love that offers many possibilities, a long-term goal that nurtures your unique gifts and makes you grow into the most confident version of yourself.

When you are physically healthy, you want to exercise, train your body, explore the world

When you are mentally healthy, you want to study, satisfy your curiosity, stretch the limits of your mind

When you are spiritually healthy, you want to bring your contribution, share your love, connect deeply with others
The more insecure you are about who you are and about the life you are living, and the more often you get angry at everyone and everything. Calm people are just people who are internally at peace.

It’s always the people who have never created or built anything that are the most judgmental of the work of others. 

People who are focused on their own product neither have the mental space nor the time for that.
If you are ambitious enough, you will naturally cultivate and grow your mental strength, because it’s impossible to clear any ambitious goal with the mindset of a victim.

There will always be one more financial opportunity, one more city to visit, one more book to study, one more skill to polish, but part of living a meaningful life is to understand that everything you do has a hidden cost, in terms of mental health, in terms of relationships with others, in terms of appreciating a lot of simple things in life that have nothing to do with money, productivity, conventional success.

Money buys sustainable peace of mind if you know how to spend it:

- no more stress due to an environment where things don't work, you can just move to another place where things work smoothly.

- no more stress due to a society where you have to argue all the time about nonsensical debates, you can just move to another place where people are smarter and wiser.

- no more stress due to not being able to afford rent, bills, reparations, groceries, safe neighborhood for your family, you can just afford better services.

- no more stress due to being forced to maintain relationships "just in case" - you are independent, you hang out with people because you enjoy their company, not because you might need them.

- no more stress due to being treated with a lot of contempt by the many insecure people who love to belittle and mock those who are in a position of weakness.

- no more stress due to worrying about whether you are productive enough, you can revert back to the carefree mindset you used to have as a child, you can read books for the pleasure of reading, you can follow your curiosity and learn what truly interests you, everything doesn't have to be about money.

- no more stress due to feeling imprisoned in a routine that doesn't allow you to become the fittest, smartest, healthiest, most creative version of yourself, you are your own boss, you are in total control of your time, you grow in the direction you decide.

In short, you just want the money in order to no longer have to think about the money at all.
Loneliness is what you feel when you don’t understand how much of a privilege having time alone is.

If you don’t understand the value of a healthy lifestyle, your goals are not ambitious enough, otherwise you would understand the necessity of becoming the most high-energy and mentally clear version of yourself.

A lot of people claim they don't have time for relationships because they have big ambitions.

The right family and friends are actually such a huge boost for your professional career.

You get to have deep and insightful conversations on a daily basis with smart people you fully trust.

It allows you to relax, you learn faster, and your mind is clearer, because you feel supported and loved; loneliness and neediness is gone, you are happy and content, and as a result, you attract a lot more opportunities in a much more effortless way.

It also gives so much more meaning to everything you do.
Relationships are actually very simple once you stop pushing your insecurities and mood changes on others.
A marriage thrives when both spouses chase their best selves together and don’t seek to control each other.

Many people become very irritable and jaded as they enter their forties and beyond because they haven't become who they wanted to be, and they are not living the life they wanted to live either. Getting older and retaining your playfulness and joie de vivre is a sign of success.

When you overthink about others, you think the whole world is overthinking about you, when truth is no one cares.

When you are insecure about yourself, you think the whole world is looking down on you, when truth is no one cares.

When you are angry at the whole world, you think everyone out there is getting angry at you, when truth is, you just get back what you project.

Your reality literally starts with who you are, and if you can change who you are, if you can figure out how to be relaxed, calm, non-judgmental, confident, then you will suddenly notice that everyone else will have significantly changed too, for the better.
